w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Consolidating the Onboarding, Reference, and Risk Engine Summaries
Consolidated final summary of the TCA three-engine operating model — Onboarding, Reference, and Risk Engines

TCA-MEMO-2026-RI-SUMMARY-001 Rev. 1 (Reference Engine summary memorandum) and the predecessor Risk Engine summary memorandum; consolidates the previously planned three individual Rev. 2 summary memos into a single anchor document
Coordinated three-engine TCA data architecture, built on a reusable 2678 Holdings onboarding kernel with entity overlays
Governs across the three engines; underlying architecture documents remain authoritative for build-phase specification

This memorandum is the final anchor summary for the TokenCap Advisors LLC three-engine operating model. It consolidates the formal architectural positions of the Onboarding Engine, the Reference Engine, and the Risk Engine into a single governing document and records the settled architectural decisions that bind all three engines as a coordinated system.
It supersedes the prior Reference Engine summary memorandum (TCA-MEMO-2026-RI-SUMMARY-001 Rev. 1) and the predecessor Risk Engine summary

replaces the previously planned individual Rev. 2 summaries. The underlying architecture documents — TCA-ONBENG-DATAARCH-2026-DRAFT-001 (Onboarding), TCA-REFENG-DATAARCH-2026-FINAL-001 (Reference), and TCA-RSKENG-DATAARCH-2026-FINAL-001 (
remain the authoritative build-phase specifications. This memorandum summarizes them and records the cross-engine resolutions but does not replace them.
The memorandum is intended for two audiences. Internally, it provides a single canonical reference for TCA personnel, technical contractors, and the CRO
on how the three engines interconnect, who has authority over what, and what is settled versus open. Externally,
where authorized, it provides a coherent description of the TCA operating model for prospective clients, counterparties, regulators, and external reviewers.

The architectural positions recorded in this memorandum are derived from the following documents. Each is treated as governing within its scope; this memorandum reconciles them at the cross-engine level.
Universal Risk Framework — canonical risk taxonomy across all sectors and all three engines
E&P Upstream Risk Framework — MVP-active vertical translation of the Universal Risk Framework
Renewables, Real Estate, and Technology Risk Frameworks — registered in the methodology registry, inactive at MVP
Three-Engine Coordination Memorandum — cross-engine boundary rules and interfaces
Texas + Kentucky E&P source inventory governing the Reference Engine MVP
Governing recordkeeping authority for the RIA-readiness posture across all three engines

organized as three coordinated engines, each with distinct database ownership, defined interfaces, and independent governance. The engines are logically separated from inception; physical separation may follow as usage grows. The architecture rests on a reusable 2678 Holdings onboarding kernel with entity-specific overlays, of which TCA is the first activation. TokenCap Miner (TCM) and TokenCap Network (TCN) are anticipated future overlays on the same kernel.

The Onboarding Engine sits at the upstream edge of the system. It creates the structured context — firm, user, engagement, package, project, permission — from which downstream engine work begins. The Reference Engine sits in the middle. It ingests external sources, produces evidence, and derives features that ground the Risk Engine's scoring activity. The Risk Engine sits at the methodology-application layer. It consumes immutable, version-pinned features from Reference and applies the Universal Risk Framework methodology to produce composite grades and Risk Packs.

The downstream consumers of the three-engine system — the Viability Opinion Report (VOR) workflow, the Risk Committee, the back-office, legal and accounting packages, and where applicable TCM and TCN workflows — receive controlled, cited outputs from each engine according to authorized scope.
Creates and maintains client firm, sponsor, user, engagement, package, project, permission, and workflow-initiation records. Built as a reusable 2678 kernel with entity overlays.
Firm, person, party, user account shadow, role binding,
, engagement, service package, fee model, legal/accounting package metadata, project, document request, uploaded document, workflow state, onboarding exception, retention policy.
sources, normalizes entities, produces evidence objects, reference packs, feature records, and benchmark outputs.

Build Source Inventory Model, source credentials metadata, raw-source and staging records, canonical entities, evidence objects, corpus chunks, vector/keyword index metadata, feature records, reference packs, provenance and audit records.
Scores and grades project risk using the Universal Risk Framework methodology through conforming vertical
Two-tier methodology registry, scoring runs, element scores, domain scores, composite scores, feature binding manifest, workflow state, exception
register, CRO override records, risk committee records, Risk Pack exports.
The Onboarding Engine is the upstream system of record for the three-engine system. It creates and maintains client

, sponsor, user, engagement, package, project, permission, and workflow-initiation records. It is built as a reusable 2678 Holdings onboarding kernel with entity-specific overlays of which TCA is the first activation. It is not a CRM, a marketing intake form, or a document folder; it is the administrative and workflow control plane for the entire three-engine system.
Create and maintain firm, person, and party records, with party roles scoped to engagements and projects.
Manage user accounts as local shadows of an external identity provider, with role bindings and project/engagement-scoped access grants.
Manage engagements, service packages, fee models, billing profiles, and legal/accounting package metadata; track engagement status from draft to closeout.
Create and govern project/opportunity records that authorize downstream Reference Engine and Risk Engine work.
Operate a document intake system with controlled upload sessions, object-storage pointers, file hashes, classifications, access rules, and retention bindings.
Run the workflow state machine that gates engagement approval, project creation, Reference job initiation, and Risk run initiation.

Maintain RIA-readiness controls: books-and-records-ready audit trail, policy and procedure acknowledgments, code-of-ethics hooks, retention class bindings, conflict checks, advisory-status flags, and compliance review records.

The Onboarding Engine workflow runs through sixteen states, from Prospect Identified through Archived. The canonical happy path is Prospect Identified → Intake Started → Party Profile Completed → Engagement Drafted → Document Intake Opened → Compliance Review Pending → Engagement Approved → Project Created → Reference Requested → Risk Requested → Active → Closed → Archived. Gating exceptions block transitions to Engagement Approved, Project Created, Reference Requested, and Risk Requested absent resolution or authorized override.

as the canonical downstream-trigger event. Reference Engine and Risk Engine activity does not begin on a drafted engagement; it begins only when the engagement transitions to Engagement Approved through the documented gating-controlled path.
The Onboarding Engine is governed by the RIA-readiness posture documented in Section 14 of TCA-ONBENG-DATAARCH-2026-DRAFT-001.
Onboarding-Engine-resident gating exceptions can be resolved by evidence or overridden by the CRO under Section 10.2 of this memorandum.
The Onboarding Engine is the system of record for all role bindings across the three-engine system, including the CRO role binding (Section 10.2).

The Reference Engine builds the authoritative external reference record. It ingests structured and document sources, normalizes entities into a canonical layer, produces evidence objects with source-tier breakdown and confidence scoring, and supplies the Risk Engine with version-pinned feature records and the VOR workflow with cited reference packs. It is not a freeform research tool, a chatbot, or a sponsor-data-only system; it is the source-grounded evidence layer between sponsor submission and risk scoring.
Maintain the Build Source Inventory Model — a governed registry of external sources organized by sector, jurisdiction, and source
Operate a credential registry and source-access grant model that records authorized access to each source without storing secrets in any engine database.
Ingest raw sources into staging records with full provenance metadata; normalize entities (operators, assets, jurisdictions, wells, leases, blocks) into a canonical entity layer.
Synthesize evidence objects, each carrying source references, confidence scoring, and a source-tier breakdown across the three tiers.
) as immutable, versioned records consumed by the Risk Engine.
Assemble reference packs for consumption by the Risk Engine, the VOR workflow, and authorized back-office consumers.
Official authoritative sources from primary regulators, courts of record, and
Texas Railroad Commission filings, Kentucky EEC well permit records, Kentucky Geological Survey datasets, court-of-record title records.
Industry-standard authoritative sources widely relied upon by regulated entities and professional service providers.
Engineering reports from recognized reserve engineering firms, recognized commodity price reference series, established trade publications with editorial accountability.
Contextual sources providing supporting information without primary authority status.
Industry analyst commentary, sector news media, sponsor-provided narrative without independent verification.
Source inventory additions, deletions, tier reclassifications, and credential changes require CRO approval.
Feature derivation logic is owned by the Reference Engine; features are produced as immutable (
) records consumed by Risk per Section 10.5.
MVP source inventory coverage is Texas and Kentucky for E&P, per the v2 Build Source Inventory workbook; expansion to Renewables, Real Estate, and Technology sources follows post-MVP.

The Risk Engine is the scoring and methodology application layer. It consumes immutable, version-pinned feature bindings from the Reference Engine and applies the Universal Risk Framework methodology through conforming vertical translations. It is not a spreadsheet replacement, an analyst-driven freeform scoring widget, or a system that ingests external evidence directly; it is the methodology-pinned scoring system that produces auditable composite grades and Risk Packs.
Maintain the two-tier methodology registry — the Universal taxonomy (TCA-UNIV-001-v1.0) as canonical parent and the four conforming vertical frameworks as registered children.
Bind feature records from Reference at scoring run initialization, immutably keyed by (
when new feature versions are published.
Execute element-level scoring (Likelihood × Impact ÷ Control Effectiveness) per the active vertical framework, with the scoring math invariant across verticals.
Compute domain composites and stage-weighted composite scores; map composite scores to TCA grade bands (A/B/C/D/S) per the Universal taxonomy.
Run the workflow state machine across ten states from Awaiting Reference to Engagement Closed, with role-based separation of duties enforced at the role assertion layer.
Maintain the exception register with gating (Minimum Data Sufficiency Control, or MDSC) and flag-able classes; gating exceptions block Draft → Submitted transitions absent resolution or CRO override.
Process CRO overrides for gating exceptions, same-human role-overlap, and methodology registry changes; CRO overrides are surfaced in Risk Pack attestation.
Assemble Risk Packs for consumption by VOR, Risk Committee, back-office, and authorized legal/accounting workflows.
Element-level scoring computes Inherent Risk = Likelihood × Impact (range 0–9), then Residual Risk = Inherent Risk ÷ Control Effectiveness (range 0–9). Likelihood and Impact are 0–3 scales;

Control Effectiveness is a 1–3 scale (1 = Weak, 2 = Adequate, 3 = Strong). Domain scores are analyst composites (1.0–5.0) across the elements of a Universal domain. The composite score is Σ (Domain Score × Stage Weight), where stage weights are vertical-specific and stage-specific. The composite score maps to a TCA grade (A = Stable, B = Core, C = Opportunistic, D = High-Risk, S = Speculative).

The Universal taxonomy defines five canonical domains — Foundational/Asset (FR), Technical & Execution (TE), Regulatory & External (RE), Commercial & Financial (CF), and Infrastructure & Market (IM). The vertical frameworks register conforming children of the Universal taxonomy with a vertical-specific name mapping for the five domains, a vertical-specific stage taxonomy, a stage-sensitive weighting profile, and a vertical-specific element registry. The methodology registry is the structural backbone of the Risk Engine and the analog of the Reference Engine's Source Inventory Model — a governed, versioned, CRO-approved control plane.
Methodology registry changes (Universal taxonomy, vertical frameworks, weighting profiles, element versions) require CRO approval with version log entry.
Gating (MDSC) exceptions can be resolved by evidence or overridden by the CRO; flag-able exceptions surface in the Risk Pack without blocking transitions.
Same-human role-overlap (analyst and reviewer on a single scoring run) is permitted only with CRO override of
= RNI_RISK_SAME_HUMAN per Section 14.1 of the Risk Engine architecture.
Section 10.1 of this memorandum; Risk Packs produced in Reference-bypass mode are non-eligible for client-facing export.

No engine begins work in an unbound state. The canonical sequence ensures that Onboarding's structured context precedes Reference's evidence work, which precedes Risk's methodology application, which precedes downstream consumer use of Risk Packs and reference packs. The Reference-bypass mode (Section 10.1) is the documented exception, narrowly scoped and CRO-authorized.
Client firm, users, engagement, package, project records created
Establishes the structured context required for any engine activity. No Reference or Risk activity yet.
Compliance review and document intake complete;
Authorizes downstream Reference and Risk activity. This is the canonical downstream-trigger event.
Reference Engine creates a scoping record; Risk Engine creates a scoring run shell in Awaiting Reference state.
Reference Engine ingests sources, normalizes entities, produces evidence and features
Reference Pack and feature records produced;
emitted. Information requests routed back through Onboarding's
Methodology version pinned; feature binding manifest recorded; analyst scoring executed under the active vertical framework.
Risk Engine workflow runs Draft → Submitted → Under Review → Approved → Locked
Role-based separation enforced at Under Review → Approved; MDSC gating enforced at Draft → Submitted; CRO overrides recorded where applied.
Risk Pack assembled and exported through controlled outbound interface
Methodology manifest, feature binding manifest, composite score, TCA grade, exception register, attestation, and audit trail reference assembled.
Downstream consumption by VOR, Risk Committee, back-office, legal/accounting as authorized
Risk Pack and Reference Pack exports are cited, permissioned, and audited. No uncontrolled copying.
All Reference jobs and Risk scoring runs transition to terminal Engagement Closed state; retention metadata written; no further state transitions permitted.
across the three engines. Underlying architecture documents will be revised where inconsistent with this section.
Onboarding creates and maintains the canonical internal record. Reference and Risk consume foreign keys and mirrored grants; they do not create or modify these records.
External source inventory, credentials metadata, raw-source records, canonical reference entities, evidence objects, reference packs, feature records
Reference owns external evidence and derived features. Risk does not ingest sources or derive features; it consumes feature records as immutable bindings.
Two-tier methodology registry, scoring runs, element scores, domain scores, composite scores, exception register, CRO override records, Risk Packs
Risk owns methodology application and scoring outputs. Reference and Onboarding do not score or apply methodology.
Onboarding is the canonical source of role
, including the CRO role binding. Reference and Risk mirror grants locally for
and enforce them at their own workflow boundaries.
Reference owns source access and credential metadata. Secrets are never stored in any engine database; only vault references and credential metadata
events. Reference receives controlled access tokens scoped to
, and document classification rules; it does not have direct file access (Section 10.4).
Common audit service; all three engines write
, and revocation writes a hash-chained, retention-tagged audit event. Audit events cannot be deleted or modified; corrections are new records (Section 10.7).
The cross-engine interface contracts are defined below at the
level. Field-level contracts are specified in the underlying architecture documents and must be honored on both sides.
, sector, jurisdiction, asset type, sponsor/operator names, controlled document access tokens, user permissions, package status, and authorized scope.
. Surfaces Reference progress on the Onboarding dashboard.
is the canonical event that authorizes Risk to begin work;
. Surfaces Risk progress on the Onboarding dashboard.
is consumed for staleness flagging, not auto-rescoring (Section 10.5).
Structured project parameters, domain-specific benchmark requests, requested evidence scope, scoring run ID, feature request (when new methodology version requires a new feature derivation).

All scoring runs, transitions, score writes, overrides, methodology lookups, source-inventory changes, role assertions, document submissions, and access grants/revocations write hash-chained audit events to the shared append-only audit store.

Risk Pack export to VOR, Risk Committee, back-office, and authorized legal/accounting workflows, with methodology manifest, feature binding manifest, composite score, TCA grade, exception register, attestation, and audit trail reference.
This section records cross-engine architectural decisions that were open at the time of the predecessor coordination memorandum and are now resolved. These resolutions
across the three engines and supersede any inconsistent specification in the underlying architecture documents, which will be revised to reflect them at their next version turn.
A transitional Reference-bypass scoring mode is approved for the Risk Engine, subject to the following constraints:

The mode permits analyst-supplied feature values when no Reference Pack is available, typically during the period between Risk Engine MVP and full Reference Engine coverage of the relevant source inventories.
A scoring run executed in Reference-bypass mode must carry an explicit
record and in the resulting Risk Pack.
Reference-bypass scoring runs require CRO authorization to initiate; the authorization is recorded in
= REFERENCE_BYPASS and is surfaced in the Risk Pack attestation.

Risk Packs produced in Reference-bypass mode are non-eligible for export to VOR, client-facing workflows, the Risk Committee for capital allocation gate decisions, or any external consumer. They may be used for internal calibration, methodology validation, and audit purposes only.
Reference-bypass scoring runs are anticipated to be retired as the Reference Engine MVP reaches full coverage of the Texas and Kentucky E&P source
. The CRO will reassess the necessity of the mode at the post-MVP review.

The CRO is designated as the super-user role across the three-engine system. This formalizes and extends the override authority specified in Risk Engine architecture Section 14.1 to the full system. The CRO is structurally independent from the operating Principal — a designated officer at an affiliated entity — and is the standing independent oversight role for the three-engine system.

Override authority over gating exceptions in any engine: Risk Engine MDSC gating exceptions; Onboarding Engine gating exceptions (unsigned engagement letter, missing required project authority, unresolved conflict check, missing policy acknowledgment, missing required sponsor document, user access not approved); Reference Engine data quality and source-access blocks.
Override authority over same-human role-overlap on Risk scoring runs (analyst and reviewer roles asserted by the same

Sole approval authority over methodology registry changes: Universal taxonomy (TCA-UNIV-001), vertical frameworks (TCA-EP-001, TCA-REN-001, TCA-RE-001, TCA-TECH-001), weighting profiles, element versions, and stage taxonomies.
Sole approval authority over Reference Engine source inventory changes: source additions, deletions, tier reclassifications, jurisdictional scope changes, and credential registry changes.
Sole authorization authority for Reference-bypass scoring mode initiation (Section 10.1).

Standing read access to the full shared append-only audit store across all three engines, including all override records, scoring runs, source provenance, document submissions, and Onboarding workflow history.

Standing participant in the annual external compliance review of CRO overrides, scoring runs, audit integrity, methodology registry changes, and source inventory changes (per Risk Engine architecture Section 14.1).
The CRO role is provisioned through the Onboarding Engine's
role. CRO override actions occur in the engine where the override is exercised and are written to the shared audit store with full traceability.
All CRO overrides require justification text (mandatory,
, and a reference to the audit event written to the shared audit
are surfaced in the relevant Risk Pack or Reference Pack attestation. Overrides cannot be retroactively deleted or modified; corrections require a new override record marked as a correction.
(on gating-controlled approval). The Reference Engine and Risk Engine consume

as the canonical event that authorizes downstream work. An engagement that has merely been created but not approved does not authorize Reference or Risk activity. The Reference Engine architecture document (Section 12 interfaces) and Risk Engine architecture document (Section 15 interfaces) will be revised to reflect this in their
turn. Contract test harnesses (Onboarding WP-7, Risk WP-2, Reference equivalent) must validate against
The Onboarding Engine is the system of record for all client and sponsor documents. Documents are stored in object storage with database-resident metadata:
. The Reference Engine and other downstream consumers do not have direct access to document objects; they receive controlled access tokens scoped to the
, and the document classification rules. The Onboarding Engine emits a
event to notify Reference that documents are available under controlled access. The Reference Engine architecture document will be revised to describe this consumption pattern explicitly.
Features are derived and persisted by the Reference Engine. The Risk Engine consumes feature records as immutable, version-pinned bindings keyed by (
) is authoritative. The Risk Engine's features schema is a single binding-manifest table (
) recording the bindings used by each scoring run.
When the Reference Engine publishes a new
, existing Risk scoring runs remain pinned to the prior version. The new version is surfaced as a staleness indicator on the Risk side via the

event. A new scoring run is required if re-scoring against new evidence is desired. Re-scoring against a new methodology version uses the same feature bindings — methodology version and feature version are independent version axes.

The TCA retention practice across all three engines and the shared audit store is seven years as the default, applied to client records, advisory records, engagement records, project records, scoring runs, reference packs, audit events, and all other material records. Five years is the minimum permitted under SEC Rule 204-2; seven years is the TCA practice. Specific record classes may be assigned different retention periods only by counsel determination, recorded in the Onboarding Engine's
table with reference to the counsel determination.
The three engines write to a single shared append-only audit store. Audit events are hash-chained as a tamper-evident sequence and cryptographically signed at material transitions:
transitions in Reference, and Approved transitions in Risk. The audit store is

by the CRO across all three engines and supports replay verification for external review under Risk Engine architecture Section 14.1. Audit events are retained for seven years per Section 10.6. Secrets are never stored in any engine database or in the audit store; only vault references and credential metadata
11. Governance, Override Authority, and Compensating Controls

The three-engine system operates with limited human staffing and heavy automation. The governance design preserves the substance of separation of duties — no single actor produces and approves the same artifact without independent oversight — while reflecting the operating reality of a small-team RIA-readiness posture. Separation of duties is enforced at the role assertion layer, not the person layer; the same human may hold the Analyst role on Project A and the Reviewer role on Project B. The CRO as super-user role provides the standing independent oversight backstop. The annual external compliance review provides
control for the small-team operating reality.
Enforced at the workflow state machine. Analyst and Reviewer role assertions on the same Risk scoring run must come from different
; same-human role-overlap requires CRO override (Section 10.2). Onboarding role bindings are scoped to engagements and projects; the same human may hold different roles on different engagements.
in Onboarding) with scoped database permissions and audit signatures. Service-identity actions are first-class events in the shared audit store.

Per Section 10.2, the CRO is the sole authority for overriding gating exceptions, same-human role-overlap, methodology changes, source inventory changes, and Reference-bypass authorization. All CRO overrides are audited and surfaced in Risk Pack and Reference Pack attestations.

The shared append-only audit store records all role assertions, service-identity actions, score writes, transitions, and overrides as hash-chained events (Section 10.7). The CRO and any external reviewer can replay the full audit sequence and verify integrity.
in Reference, Approved transitions in Risk — write cryptographically signed records. Signing keys are held by the approving identity, not the analyst identity.

Standing compensating control. An annual external compliance review covers CRO overrides, scoring runs, audit integrity, methodology registry changes, and source inventory changes. Scope and provider selection are open items under CRO and Counsel ownership.
12. Texas + Kentucky E&P Joint MVP Scope

The MVP for the three-engine system is the joint Texas + Kentucky E&P proof. The Onboarding Engine MVP creates and gates engagement and project records sufficient to initiate Reference Engine and Risk Engine work for Texas and Kentucky E&P projects. The Reference Engine MVP produces source-grounded evidence and feature records from the v2 Build Source Inventory (Texas RRC, Kentucky EEC, Kentucky Geological Survey). The Risk Engine MVP scores those projects under TCA-EP-001-v1.0 as a conforming translation of TCA-UNIV-001-v1.0.
TokenCap Advisors LLC overlay on the 2678 Holdings onboarding kernel. TCM and TCN overlays are placeholders only, with kernel design preserving future activation without schema redesign.
E&P upstream oil and gas only. TCA-REN-001, TCA-RE-001, and TCA-TECH-001 are registered in the methodology registry but inactive for scoring at MVP.

Texas and Kentucky. Multi-jurisdiction extensibility is proven by the joint MVP, which forces the Build Source Inventory Model pattern, jurisdictional identifier normalization, and canonical entity layer to operate across two jurisdictions from inception.
TCA-EP-001-v1.0 only, across all six E&P stages (Virgin Exploration, Prospect Defined, Permitted/Drill-Ready, Drilled & Tested, Development, Producing/PDP).
Two complete end-to-end runs — one Texas E&P project and one Kentucky E&P project — each progressing from Onboarding intake through
, Reference Engine ingestion and feature publication, Risk Engine scoring run, and Risk Pack export, with full audit trail across the shared append-only audit store.

Production-grade TCM KYC/token issuance, TCN network onboarding, full Renewables/Real Estate/Technology onboarding packages, deep accounting system integration, e-signature execution workflow, public self-service marketplace accounts, and investor portal functionality.

The current GoDaddy VPS is a transitional/staging asset. It may support lightweight prototypes, thin admin/UI testing, and deployment validation across all three engines. It is not the target production data infrastructure for any engine.
Managed PostgreSQL with logical schema separation.
(for corpus chunk embeddings). Logically separated from Onboarding and Risk.
S3/GCS/Azure Blob equivalent. Durable storage for submitted documents (owned by Onboarding) and export artifacts (Risk Packs, Reference Packs).
Google Workspace/Cloud Identity, Auth0, or Cognito equivalent. Owns authentication; Onboarding mirrors user identities as
records and owns role bindings and access grants.
Cloud secret manager or vault, shared across all three engines. API keys, source credentials, webhook secrets, object-storage credentials are stored only as vault references in engine databases.
Append-only event store with hash-chained sequences, cryptographic signing at material transitions, and seven-year retention.
by the CRO across all three engines.
REST/webhooks for MVP; event bus/queue post-MVP. Idempotency keys required on all outbound integration events.
Containerized service deployment for production. Staging may continue to use the current VPS.
Automated snapshots for all engine databases, object versioning for object storage, audit-store replication, and tested restore procedures.

Cloud platform selection (AWS, GCP, Azure, or hybrid) is an open item recorded in Section 15; the selection must be common across all three engines to support the shared audit store, shared secret manager, and infrastructure consistency.
14. Acceptance Posture Across the Three Engines

The acceptance criteria below apply across the three engines as a system. Engine-specific acceptance criteria are recorded in the respective underlying architecture documents (Onboarding Section 24, Reference Section equivalent, Risk Section 20).
Architectural separation: Onboarding, Reference, and Risk records are logically separated with documented interfaces; no collapse into one uncontrolled database.
record can initiate a Reference Engine job, which produces feature records that a Risk Engine scoring run can bind and use to produce a Risk Pack — end-to-end with full audit trail.
CRO super-user authority: Override authority is operational across all three engines, with override records audited and surfaced in attestations.
Reference-bypass mode: Operational with explicit flag, CRO authorization, and non-eligibility for client-facing export.
Feature immutability: Feature bindings are version-pinned at scoring run initialization; staleness flagging is surfaced; new methodology version re-scoring uses the same bindings.
Shared audit store: All material events from all three engines write to the shared store as hash-chained, signed where applicable, and retention-tagged to seven years.
Controlled-access document model: Reference and other downstream consumers do not have direct file access; access is mediated by Onboarding-issued tokens.
Texas + Kentucky joint MVP: Two complete end-to-end runs (one Texas, one Kentucky) executed with full Reference Pack and Risk Pack export.

Infrastructure realism: Production targets managed PostgreSQL, object storage, secret manager, shared audit store, identity provider, and containerized services. Current VPS documented as staging only.
RIA-readiness: Books-and-records-ready audit trail, policy and procedure acknowledgments,
bindings, and compliance review records implemented across the three engines.
The following items remain open and are tracked for resolution. They do not block MVP build but should be settled before the system moves to multi-tenant or production-scale operation.
AWS, GCP, Azure, or hybrid. Must be common across all three engines for shared audit store, shared secret manager, and infrastructure consistency.
Google Workspace/Cloud Identity, Auth0, or Cognito. Must support project/engagement-scoped roles.
Scope is defined (CRO overrides, scoring runs, audit integrity, methodology registry changes, source inventory changes); provider selection is open.
RIA registration timing and Form ADV workflow
The system is built RIA-ready; the registration decision and the associated Form ADV workflow are open.
TCM and TCN overlay activation timing
The kernel design preserves future activation without schema redesign; the activation timing for each overlay is open.
Whether the TCA Grade emits a confidence qualifier derived from feature confidence (e.g., "TCA-B (Moderate Confidence)") or remains a flat grade.
Whether the indicative IRR band from the TCA grade band is shown in the Risk Pack to downstream consumers or remains
Existing completed assessments (Red River Haynesville, Henderson County, Escopeta/Trinidad South). Recommendation: archived as historical,
with Red River retained as the canonical sample for new-system smoke-testing.
VOR is referenced as a downstream consumer across all three engines but is not separately architected. Consideration of a fourth architecture document for VOR is open.

How Risk surfaces a request for a new feature derivation to Reference when a new methodology version requires it; SLA, requestor role, scoring run pause/proceed-under-flag during the request lifecycle.
Final retention schedule by record type
Default is seven years per Section 10.6; record-class-specific deviations require counsel determination.

The TCA three-engine operating model is governed by this memorandum at the system level and by the three underlying architecture documents at the build-phase specification level. The Onboarding Engine is

upstream system of record for client, user, engagement, package, project, permission, and workflow-initiation records, built on a reusable 2678 Holdings onboarding kernel with entity overlays. The Reference Engine is the source-grounded evidence layer that produces canonical entities, evidence objects, reference packs, and immutable feature records. The Risk Engine is the methodology application layer that consumes version-pinned features and produces auditable composite grades and Risk Packs under the Universal Risk Framework. The three engines are coordinated through documented event interfaces, write to a shared append-only audit store, and operate under CRO super-user authority for all override decisions.

The Texas + Kentucky E&P joint MVP is the first proof of the three-engine model end-to-end: an Onboarding-created engagement and project, scored under TCA-EP-001-v1.0 as a conforming translation of TCA-UNIV-001-v1.0, using Reference Engine evidence and features drawn from the Texas RRC, Kentucky EEC, and Kentucky Geological Survey source inventories, producing a Risk Pack that progresses through controlled outbound interfaces to downstream consumption. The Reference-bypass mode is the documented exception, narrowly scoped, CRO-authorized, and non-eligible for client-facing export.

The three architecture documents (TCA-ONBENG-DATAARCH-2026-DRAFT-001, TCA-REFENG-DATAARCH-2026-FINAL-001, TCA-RSKENG-DATAARCH-2026-FINAL-001) will be revised at their next version turns to align with this memorandum where inconsistencies exist — principally the

event distinction, the controlled-access document model, the inclusion of the CRO role in the Onboarding role inventory, and the standardized seven-year retention across all engine records. The coordination memorandum (TCA-MEMO-2026-3ENG-COORD-001) will be revised to mark the previously open feature derivation boundary as closed and to incorporate the settled decisions of this anchor document.
This memorandum is the final anchor for the TCA three-engine system as of June 2026.
